name: Canada (1868–1921)
description: A red field with the Union Flag in the upper hoist canton and, in the centre of the fly, a composite shield bearing the arms of the Canadian provinces. The shield was modified as new provinces entered Confederation, accreting quarterings until its design became unwieldy.
history: The Canadian Red Ensign was first flown in 1868, shortly after Confederation, when the Admiralty authorised Canadian-registered ships to fly the British Red Ensign defaced with the composite arms of the original four provinces (Ontario, Quebec, Nova Scotia, New Brunswick). Although primarily a merchant ensign, it was widely used on land as Canada's de facto national flag. The shield was repeatedly modified as new provinces joined: Manitoba, British Columbia, and Prince Edward Island arms were added in 1870, 1871, and 1873 respectively. By the early 20th century the design held seven and then nine provincial arms in a single shield. In 1921 King George V granted Canada its own coat of arms (red maple leaves on a white base, surmounted by quarterings of the four founding kingdoms), and the Red Ensign was redrawn to use this new shield, ending the period of the proliferating provincial composite arms.

features.symbolism: the British Red Ensign, used by ships of the merchant marine and unofficially as the Dominion of Canada's national flag
the Union Flag, denoting Canada's status as a self-governing dominion of the British Empire

features.role: Composite arms of the four original provinces (Ontario, Quebec, Nova Scotia, New Brunswick), to which arms of additional provinces were progressively added as they joined Confederation
features.symbolism: the provinces of the Dominion of Canada
